segments.s741-g129: هرآن functions as a distributive determiner meaning 'each/such' (classical usage); گوهری is a noun 'jewel' with the adjectival/derivational element کش attached after it (کش here is an agentive/derivational element forming a compound), and بهاخوار is a compound adjective meaning 'cheap / low‑priced'. بود is the past copula 'was'. Word order is noun + compound adjective + copula (typical older Persian).
segments.s741-g130: کمابیش is an adverb 'more or less / about'; هفتاد دینار is a numeral + currency phrase 'seventy dinars'; بود is the past tense copula 'was', so the clause states an approximate past value.
segments.s741-g131: بسی here functions as an indefinite quantifier 'many / much' (classical use); نیز = 'also'; بگذشت is past 'passed/exceeded' (here 'exceeded a number'); بر with a numeral means 'over / beyond' and governs هفتصد 'seven hundred'. Together: 'many also exceeded seven hundred.'
segments.s741-g132: همی‌گیر is an adjective meaning 'widespread / prevalent' describing the items; زین گونه is an older idiom 'of this kind' (زین + گونه = 'this kind/type'); از introduces the scope 'of (or from)'; نیک و بد 'good and bad' lists the two qualities. Word order places the descriptive phrase after the numeral clause to qualify what exceeded seven hundred.
segments.s741-g133: بسی = 'many'; سرخ گوگرد = 'red sulfur' (adjective before noun); بد کش بها is a compound cluster where بد qualifies کش (derivational element) and بها = 'price/value' — the cluster describes these sulfurs as being of low/poor value or cheaply priced. The sequence follows classical Persian adjective‑noun order and uses compound elements to express quality.
segments.s741-g134: ندانست is the past negative of دانستن 'did not know'; کس in negative context means 'no one' (though listed also as 'someone'); مایه and منتها are two nouns here meaning 'amount/substance' and 'limit/end' respectively. The clause literally: 'no one knew the amount and the limit.' Word order is verb (negative past) + subject (indefinite 'no one') + objects.
segments.s741-g135: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause 'that / when'; روشن is an adjective 'bright' or a verbal/adjectival participle 'making bright'; بدی here in the line functions as part of the adjectival/verb phrase (classical wordings sometimes use short forms), but in the lexicon entry it's listed as 'evil' — in context it belongs to the older poetic construction meaning 'you gave/you were' (see usage), so the whole sequence reads 'that (you) made bright'; در شب تیره = 'in the dark night'; چهر = 'face/appearance' (object). Word order places the time/place phrase after the verb phrase, as is common in Ferdowsi's diction.
segments.s741-g136: چوناهید is a temporal/concessive particle meaning 'when/as you came' or 'as you appeared'; رخشان is an adjective 'radiant'; شدی is the past tense of شدن 'you became' (second person singular past); بر سپهر = 'upon the sky/heavens' with بر as a locative/ 'on'. The clause says that on appearing you became radiant on the heavens, using the past narrative tense.
segments.s741-g65: ز هر کشوری is a prepositional phrase (ز = 'from' in poetic register + هر کشوری = 'every/each country'). مهتران is the direct object and carries the accusative marker را which in classical/poetic spelling is attached to the verb as رابخواند (i.e. را خواند). رابخواند is a 3rd‑person singular past/narrative form meaning 'he summoned/called together.'
segments.s741-g66: وزان is the fused phrase و از آن ('and from that/and of that'). تخت is 'throne'; چندی is a quantifier meaning 'a few' or 'some'; سخنها is the plural 'words/speeches'. براند is a classical poetic 3rd‑person form with the prefix بر‑ (here functioning like برآورد/بَرآورد in sense 'bring forth/utter') — in context it means 'he uttered/expressed' a few words. Word order is Subject (implied) + adverbial/locative (وزان تخت) + quantified object (چندی سخنها) + verb (براند).
segments.s741-g67: ازیشان is از ایشان ('from/of them' = those people as the affected group). فراوان here is adverbial 'in great numbers/abundantly'. شکسته is a predicative adjective meaning 'broken/humbled'. بیافت is an older narrative/past form of افتادن ('to fall' / 'befall') used here as 'fell' or 'befell' — together the clause reads 'many of them fell humbled.'
segments.s741-g68: به شادی is an adverbial phrase 'in joy' or 'with rejoicing' (به + noun). سوی is a directional preposition 'toward'; گرد کردن is a light/compound verbal idea 'to gather/assemble' (گرد = round/assemble + کردن as the auxiliary). شتافت is the past/narrative 'he/they hastened/rushed.' The clause as a whole is an adverbial motion: '(they) hastened, in joy, toward assembling.'
segments.s741-g69: بیاورد is a classical 3rd‑person singular past/narrative form meaning 'he brought' (often used in storytelling). پس is temporal/adverbial 'then' or 'afterward'. تخت شاه اردشیر is a genitival sequence 'the throne of King Ardashir' (classical Persian often strings nouns without an explicit ezāfe). The clause therefore states 'Then he brought the throne of King Ardashir.'
segments.s741-g70: ز ایران هر آنکس که begins a relative/indefinite clause: ز ایران = 'from Iran', هر آنکس که = 'whoever/anyone who'. بد is an attributive/adverbial 'bad/ill' modifying the following epithet. تیزویر is an archaic/compound epithet; here it is a descriptive compound attached to that 'whoever' and is left untranslated literal as an epithet in the line (an obscure or poetic compound). Grammatically, this segment sets up 'from Iran whoever was [descriptor] ...' and continues afterward.
segments.s741-g71: بهم بر زدند is an idiomatic verbal phrase: بهم = 'one another/to each other' and بر زدند is the 3rd‑person plural past of زدن with the prefix بر‑ (here 'to strike/attack'); together it means 'they struck/assailed one another' or more loosely 'they fought among themselves'. آن سزاوار تخت is a noun phrase 'that deserving/fit throne' (آن = 'that', سزاوار = 'deserving/fit', تخت = 'throne'), the object/target of the action.
segments.s741-g72: به هنگام is a temporal prepositional phrase 'at the time of' (به + هنگام). آن شاه پیروزبخت = 'that fortunate/fortunate king' (آن = 'that/then', شاه = 'king', پیروزبخت = 'fortunate/prosperous'), forming the time reference 'at the time of that fortunate king.' Grammatically this is an adverbial temporal phrase modifying the preceding clause.
segments.s741-g153: چو = 'when' (temporal conjunction). بر تخت پیروزه is a prepositional phrase (preposition بر + noun تخت + postpositive adjective پیروزه), meaning 'on the turquoise throne' — classical Persian often places adjectives after the noun. بودی نشست is a poetic/inverted combination: بودی is the 2nd-person past of بودن ('you were') used together with the verbal/participial notion of نشست ('seated/sat'), so the whole unit means 'you were seated' / 'you sat' (poetic word order: auxiliary before main verbal form).
segments.s741-g154: خردمند بودی is straightforward predication: adjective خردمند + second‑person past بودی = 'you were wise.' و connects the two predicates. مهترپرست is a compound noun/adjective (مهتر + پرست) meaning one who flatters or reveres the great; it functions here as a predicate noun describing the same subject ('you were ... and a flatterer/of chiefs').
segments.s741-g155: چو رفتی = 'when you went' (چو = 'when', رفتی = 2nd‑person past of رفتن). به دستوری here means 'by order / under command' (به + دستوری), where دستوری has the sense 'commanding / by order' and modifies the following noun; رهنمای is a noun 'guide' (object/role). Together the phrase means 'when you went as a guide under orders' (わ 'به' is used adverbially to mark the manner/authority).
segments.s741-g156: مگر here is the rhetorical/expectant مِگر = 'perhaps / did you (then) ...?' introducing a question. یافتی is 2nd‑person past of یافتن 'did you find/obtain'. نزد means 'in the presence of / near (the court of)'; پرویز is the proper name; جای = 'place/position'. The clause asks whether, upon going as a guide, you managed to obtain a position at Parviz's side.
segments.s741-g157: یکی before a noun here is the indefinite 'a certain / one' introducing the garment. جامه افکنده is a participial phrase: جامه (garment) + افکنده (past participle 'thrown/put on') = 'a garment put on/ thrown over (him)'. بد زربفت is a compound adjectival phrase: بد ('poorly / bad') modifying زربفت ('gold‑brocaded'), so 'poorly gold‑brocaded' or 'bad gold‑brocade.'
segments.s741-g158: برش بود is a short predicate meaning 'it was on him' (بر + ـش = 'on him'), with بود as the past copula 'was.' وبالاش پنجاه و هفت is terse/elliptical poetic phrasing: و + بالاش (بالا + ـش, 'and above it / on top of it') + پنجاه و هفت (the number 'fifty‑seven'). The line lists an item on him and then adds that 'fifty‑seven' (something) were above it — the exact referent is condensed in the poetry.
segments.s741-g159: بگوهر is a verbal/adjectival formation meaning 'set with gems' (بـ + گوهر), used here as a modifier. همه ریشه‌ها is a plural nominal phrase 'all the roots/fringes' (ریشه‌ها used in textile sense = fringes/ends) and بافته is the past participle 'woven' — together 'woven with gems on all the fringes.' Word order is adjective/verb after the noun, typical of classical Persian.
segments.s741-g160: زبر here has the sense 'upper / above' (zebr/zaabar = on top); شوشه is a textile term 'tassel / plume'; زر = 'gold'; برو تافته is a compound participial description (تافته = 'woven' / 'radiant'), describing that the gold tassels above were finely/well woven. The sequence is a compact ornamental description: 'above, gold tassels were richly/well woven.'
segments.s741-g1: کنون is a fronted temporal adverb meaning 'now.' داستان گوی is a noun phrase 'story-teller' (داستان + گوی), and در داستان is a locative prepositional phrase 'in the tale' that modifies the noun phrase; the main verb is omitted here (a common poetic ellipsis). Note that گوی functions as 'speaker/teller' (an older usage of the root گفتن).
segments.s741-g2: ازان introduces the referent 'of those/of that,' linking to what the teller will speak about. یک دل و یک زبان is an idiomatic coordinate phrase 'one in heart and one in tongue' (unity of feeling and speech); ویک here is the contracted orthography of 'و یک' ('and one') and functions as a numeral conjunction in poetry. راستان is an adjectival/epithet meaning 'upright/righteous' modifying the preceding phrase.
segments.s741-g3: ز تختی که is a poetic 'from/of a throne that...' introducing a relative clause (که). خوانی here functions as a noun-like element 'a spread/banquet' (a poetic use of خوان) and ورا 'thereon/thither' links it to the following noun phrase طاق دیس 'an arch/vault of platters.' Word order places the relative clause elements after the head تختی.
segments.s741-g4: که begins the relative clause continuing the description. بنهاد is an archaic past verb 'he placed/put/established' and follows the relative marker; پرویز is the agent (proper name) and دراسپریس is a locative proper name (place) indicating where the placing occurred. The clause is a typical subject–verb–locative sequence in past tense.
segments.s741-g5: سرمایه آن is a nominal phrase 'its source/capital' (سرمایه here = 'source/foundation'), followed by ز + proper name to indicate provenance: 'from Zahhak.' بود is the past copula 'was.' The clause states origin with a simple past copular construction.
segments.s741-g6: که introduces a relative clause 'who...' describing ضحاک. ناپارسا and ناپاک are adjectival predicates ('impious' and 'impure/unclean'); each predicate is paired with the copula بود in the past for emphasis and poetic parallelism. The coordinating و simply links the two adjectival clauses.
segments.s741-g7: بگاهی که is a temporal subordinating phrase 'at a time when' (بگاهی = 'at the time of/once'), followed by the past verb رفت 'he went' and گرد 'about/around' forming the clause 'when Afrīdūn went about.' The sequence places the temporal phrase before the action, a common poetic order.
segments.s741-g8: وزان ('and from that/from those') links the next event to the previous clause. تازیان is the plural 'Arabs'; نام مردی is a genitival noun phrase 'the name of a man' (نام the head, مردی specifying whose name). ببرد is a past-form of بردن ('he took/carried'), here used idiomatically to mean 'took/learned/heard' the name — a polyvalent classical usage where بردن can mean 'to take, obtain, or gain (a name or reputation)'.
segments.s741-g17: This is a compact, somewhat archaic word order. درم (dirhams) is the direct object placed before the verb; داد is the simple past 3rd-person 'gave'. مر is an archaic short form of 'mā(ra)' = 'to me' (indirect object). جهن را is the topical/definite-marked subject/name (the particle را marks the name as a definite object/topic). سی‌هزار is the numeral quantifying the amount (here linked to درم).
segments.s741-g18: Numeric phrase: یکی 'one' precedes the noun تاج (crown), with the adjective زرین 'golden' following تاج in typical classical Persian order. و links the first item to the second, دو 'two' quantifies گوشوار (earring/ornament). There is no plural suffix because the numeral provides the plurality.
segments.s741-g19: همان 'the same/that' identifies a previously mentioned pledge (عهد). ساری و آمل are place names (cities) serving as the indirect objects/destinations of the verb. نوشت is the simple past 'wrote' (here 'wrote to' or 'addressed'). In classical Persian the destination can follow the object and the verb.
segments.s741-g20: که introduces a relative/clause ('that'/'which'). مرز 'border' is the locational noun; منشور او 'his charter/inscription' places the possessor after the noun (classical genitive order). چون here means 'like/as', making a simile with بهشت 'paradise.' The word بد appears here in an archaic locative/idiomatic function before مرز (the provided sense list does not include this usage, so it is treated as irregular/archaic in meaning).
segments.s741-g21: بدانگه is an adverbial temporal phrase 'at that time/thereupon'. که introduces the subordinate clause 'when'. ایران is the subject (the land/kingdom); به + ایرج marks the recipient/beneficiary 'to Irāj' (the kingdom passed to Irāj). رسید is the past tense verb 'reached/was attained' indicating transfer/arrival.
segments.s741-g22: کزان = 'from which/from that (time/place)' introduces a continuation: نامداران 'the renowned/illustrious men' is the plural noun; وی is the possessive/pronominal 'his' referring back to the named person; آمد پدید is a two-part verbal phrase meaning 'came forth/appeared' (آمد = came, پدید = manifest/appear).
segments.s741-g23: A title + name sequence: جهاندار 'world-ruler/sovereign' functions as an epithet modifying شاه 'king', followed by the proper name آفریدون. سه چیز 'three things' is the direct-object phrase (numeral + noun) that will be taken up by the verb in the next segment.
segments.s741-g24: بران is an archaic locative/relational particle 'upon that/for it' (pointing back to the kingship). پادشاهی 'kingship/kingdom' is the object. برافزود is the past tense 'he added/added on' (بر + افزود), and نیز 'also/as well' adds emphasis that this was an additional action.
segments.s741-g49: چنین is a demonstrative/adverb meaning “thus/so” used to qualify تابگاه. تابگاه is a compound poetic noun here meaning a radiant palace/ pavilion (poetic/archaic). سکندر is the subject (proper name). رسید is the simple past (3rd sing.) of رسیدن “to arrive/reach.” Word order is slightly inverted for poetic effect: the qualifying phrase precedes the subject and then the past verb completes the clause.
segments.s741-g50: ز + شاهان = “among/of the kings” (ز used in its poetic prepositional sense). هر ... که is a universal quantifier with a relative clause: هر آنکس که = “whoever/whomever who.” آن here temporal/pronominal = “that/then (at that time).” گاه is the noun “time/occasion.” دید is the past tense “saw.” The clause therefore means “whoever among the kings saw it then.”
segments.s741-g51: همی‌برفزودی is an archaic/poetic verb form of برفزودن “to add/increase,” with همی marking immediacy or continuative aspect; here it functions as a past action “(he) added/augmented.” برو is formally the imperative “go,” but in classical poetic usage it can appear adverbially/colloquially as a discourse particle meaning “thereupon/then” (hence its effect here). چند چیز = “a few things / several items” (چند as an indefinite quantifier + چیز “thing”).
segments.s741-g52: ز introduces a list of materials (“of / from”). زر, سیم, عاج name materials in a standard treasure list: gold, silver, ivory. و is the conjunction “and.” شیز is an obscure/rare term here (listed as such); context makes it another valuable material. The repetition of ز for each item is a poetic/archaic coordinating pattern.
segments.s741-g53: مر is an archaic/discourse element appearing before the object (here used as a particle — see note). آن را marks a definite object (آن “that” + object marker را). سکندر is the subject; همه پاره کرد is a verbal phrase built from پاره کردن “to tear to pieces” with همه emphasising totality (“all/entirely”) and کرد as the auxiliary/main past verb (3rd pl./sg. past form depending on context). The clause is S (سکندر) — O (آن را) — V (پاره کرد) with poetic placement of the particle.
segments.s741-g54: ز بی دانشی is a prepositional/causal phrase meaning “out of/for lack of knowledge” (ز = “from/because of,” بی = “without,” دانشی = “knowledge”). کار یکباره کرد is an idiomatic sequence: کار here = “deed/act,” یکباره = “suddenly/at once,” and کرد is the past verb “he did” — together “he did the deed at once.”
segments.s741-g55: بسی از بزرگان = “many/of the great ones” (بسی used in the older sense of a large amount/number + از + plural noun بزرگان “the great/nobles”). نهان داشتند uses نهان “hidden” with داشتند (past tense of داشتن) meaning “they kept/held [it] hidden.” The object is implicit (the thing mentioned previously).
segments.s741-g56: همی is a continuative/discourse adverb (“still/just/indeed” in classical usage). دست بر دست بگذاشتند is an idiom: literally “they put hand upon hand,” i.e. they folded their hands / sat idly by. بگذاشتند is the past (3rd pl.) of گذاشتن “to place,” so the whole phrase describes a continuing/passive-inert response.
segments.s741-g41: یکایک 'one by one' is an adverbial modifier placed before the imperative ببین ('look/see') to stress careful, successive examination. تا here introduces a subordinate indirect question ('see what...') rather than temporal 'until'; چه خواهی فزود is an indirect question with خواهی (2nd-person modal/future auxiliary 'you will') governing the classical verb form فزود ('add/increase').
segments.s741-g42: پس از مرگ is a temporal prepositional phrase 'after the death'; ما را که is the object 'us' (ما) plus the enclitic object-marking را fused with the relative/ interrogative که (rendered here as 'who'), forming 'who will (do X) to us'. خواهد ستود is a future construction: خواهد (auxiliary 'will') + ستود ('praise'), yielding 'will praise.'
segments.s741-g43: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when' introducing the clause; جا + ماسپ here form the proper name جاماسپ (Jamasp) — the two elements are written separately in the line but function as the name of the subject. آن تخت را بنگرد (here رابنگرید) means 'looked at that throne' with the object marker را attached to the verb (رابنگرید). The verb is third-person narrative/past (classical epic tense) 'looked/regarded.'
segments.s741-g44: بدید is simple past 'he saw.' از در is a spatial expression 'at/from the door(gate)' where در is used as 'door, gate.' گنجِ دانش is a genitive phrase 'treasure of knowledge' (گنج modified by دانش), and کلید is the direct object 'the key' placed at the end in the normal SOV/order.
segments.s741-g45: برو is an imperative 'go.' بر شمار is a verbal/adverbial phrase 'to count' or 'upon counting' (بر + شمار) directing the action toward enumeration; سپهر بلند 'the lofty heaven/sky' is the object (or scope) of that counting, with بلند as an adjectival qualifier after the noun (typical adjective placement).
segments.s741-g46: همی‌کرد is an imperfect/habitual narrative tense 'he would do / was doing.' پیدا here functions as 'make manifest / reveal' (predicate element), so همی‌کرد پیدا = 'he would make manifest.' چه و چون و چند is a fixed triplet of interrogatives meaning 'what and how and how many' (i.e., content, manner, and number).
segments.s741-g47: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' introducing the lower bound of a range; کیوان (Kīyvān) names the planet/Saturn as the starting point. همه نقشها 'all the figures/designs' is the direct object (note plural), and تا به ماه marks the upper bound 'as far as the Moon' (تا ... به indicating a range 'from X to Y').
segments.s741-g48: بران تخت ('on that throne') uses the archaic demonstrative/locative بران = بر آن 'upon that.' کرد او is the verb 'he made/placed' (کرد) with the subject pronoun او; به فرمان شاه is an instrumental/agent phrase 'by the command of the king' (به + فرمان شاه). The overall order follows epic Persian patterns (prepositional/adjunct phrases surrounding the verb).
segments.s741-g81: نفرمود is نفر + فرمود, the negated past of فرمود (he said/commanded) — so ‘he did not say/command.’ تا introduces an extent/point of time: تا یک زمان = 'until/for a short while.' دم زدند is the verbal phrase دم زدن (literally 'to breathe') used idiomatically here to mean 'to speak/utter (a word)'; زدند is past plural. Together: 'he remained silent until, for a short time, they spoke.'
segments.s741-g82: بدو = به + او (to him / unto him). سال here expresses a duration ('for a year'). تا again marks extent/duration. تخت = 'throne'. برهم + زدند: برهم زدن = to disturb/overturn/upset; زدند is past plural. The clause says that, for a year (relative to him), they were striking/upsetting the throne.
segments.s741-g83: چوبر is a temporal particle meaning 'when / as soon as.' پای کردند is a causative/perfective construction: پای (base/foot) + کردند (they made/placed) — 'they set the base.' تخت بلند is a noun phrase 'the lofty/tall throne.' Word order is typical: temporal particle + verb phrase + noun phrase result.
segments.s741-g84: درخشنده شد is adjective + شد (became) — 'became shining/resplendent.' روی here means 'face/appearance' (not the preposition); بخت بلند is a compound meaning 'high/lofty fortune' (بخت = fate/fortune, بلند = lofty). The clause links resultively: the throne's face became shining and a sign of exalted luck.
segments.s741-g85: برش here functions as a possessive/measure noun 'its measure/section/profile' (بر + ش). بود is past 'was.' بالای = 'above / over.' صد = 'a hundred' (numeric). شاه رش appears as a compound measurement name; grammatically this is a genitive/measure clause: 'its measure was above a hundred shah‑rash.'
segments.s741-g86: چو = 'when / as' introduces a comparative/conditional clause. هفتاد رش = 'seventy [units called] rash.' برنهی is lexically uncertain in isolation in this passage (the form appears to mean something like 'you put/lay/remove/compare' here), and ازبرش literally is ازبر + ش where ازبر can mean 'by heart / from memory' but can also be part of a locative/ablative expression; the exact idiomatic sense in the poem is obscure. Grammatically: this is a subordinate clause introduced by چو describing a relation involving seventy rash and 'برش.'
segments.s741-g87: صد و بیست رش = 'one hundred and twenty rash' (numeric measure). نیز = 'also / likewise.' پهناش = پهنا + ش, 'its breadth.' بود is past 'was.' The clause states the breadth as an additional measured dimension: 'its breadth was also 120 rash.'
segments.s741-g88: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'which/that.' پهناش 'its breadth' is the subject; کمتر = 'less (in amount/degree).' ز is the poetic form of از, 'than / from.' بالاش = بالا + ش, 'its height/upper part.' بود is the copula 'was.' So: 'which (its breadth) was less than its height.'
segments.s741-g105: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a clause. هنگامه is a noun 'moment/season' and Tir ماه is the month-name 'Tir' with ماه ('month') after it (modifier + head: تیر ماه = the month Tir). آمدی is an archaic/epic past intransitive 'came/arrived' used impersonally here ('there came'); the -ی ending is part of Ferdowsi's older verb form rather than a 2nd-person ending.
segments.s741-g106: گه functions as a noun 'an occasion/time' (here 'a season' or 'a time of'), listing two kinds of times: میوه 'fruit' (harvest/fruitfulness) and جشنگاه 'festival/occasion of celebration'. The copular verb آمدی repeats the impersonal past 'came' to complete the parallelism: 'a time of fruit came and a time of festivity came.'
segments.s741-g107: سوی + noun phrase means 'toward' (سوی میوه و باغ = toward the fruit and the garden). بودیش is a fused copula 'بود' with the enclitic 'ایش' (to/for him) meaning 'it was to him' or simply 'his ... was'; روی 'face/gaze' is placed at the end as the predicate nominative: 'his face was toward the fruit and the garden.'
segments.s741-g108: بدان here functions as 'to that' (classical be + an = 'to/at that'), introducing purpose. تا introduces purpose 'so that / in order that'. بیابد is a subjunctive/jussive third-person singular of یافتن 'to find' ('might find'). زهرمیوه is a compound 'poisonous fruit' and بوی is its object 'scent/smell' — the clause means 'so that he might find the scent of the poisonous fruit.'
segments.s741-g109: زمستان 'winter' is the time noun and که links the temporal clause ('when'). بودی is the past copula 'was' (older form with -ی). گه here means 'at times' or 'sometimes', and باد و نم are the weather items 'wind and moisture/dew' describing conditions during that season.
segments.s741-g110: بر آن تخت = 'on that throne' (بر = on/upon, آن = that, تخت = throne). برکس is a proper/name (foreign name) attached to تخت (the throne of Braks). نبودی is the negative past 'were not' (نبود + -ی), and دژم is the predicate adjective 'sullen/grim' describing the addressee's demeanor on that throne.
segments.s741-g111: همه طاقها 'all the vaults/arches' is the subject phrase. بود is the past copula 'were' and بسته is the participial adjective 'closed' describing their state. ازار is a noun 'harm/annoyance/distress' stating the effect or condition (here rendered 'distress' or 'nuisance').
segments.s741-g112: ز here is the poetic form of از meaning 'from' or 'because of'; خز و سمور are materials/furs ('khaz' = velvet/fur, سمور = sable/fur) causing the disturbance. از در شهریار literally 'from the king's door/gate' (از = from/of, در = door/gate here, شهریار = the king), so the phrase locates the disturbance at the ruler's gate.
segments.s741-g33: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. آمد is a past tense verb 'came/arrived' (3rd sg.). به introduces the indirect/direct object 'to' (he came to); the proper name کیخسرو follows the preposition. نیک‌بخت is a postpositive epithet 'fortunate' attached to the name (poetic epithet after the name).
segments.s741-g34: فراوان is an adverb 'plentifully/much' modifying the following verb. بیفزود is a past 3rd-person singular verb 'he added/augmented' (classical form; the initial ب- is a verbal prefix). بالای is a locative phrase 'on/upon the top of' and تخت is the noun 'throne' as the locus/item affected by the adding.
segments.s741-g35: برین here is analyzable as بر + این (on/at this), with بر 'on/upon' and ین representing 'این' (this); هم adds 'also/too' or 'the same'. نشان means 'sign/emblem/insignia'. تا introduces a limit/result 'until' and به لهراسپ شد literally 'became to Lohrasp' — classical use of شد 'became/was (turned over to)' to indicate coming into someone's hands.
segments.s741-g36: وزو is an archaic/poetic connective here ('و زو' = 'and from that/and it') — the provided lexical sense list is not a direct fit, so its precise dictionary sense is uncertain in the given list. همچنان 'in the same way/likewise' links the two clauses. تا ... شد repeats the 'until it became/was passed to' construction, this time with گشتاسپ (Gushtasp) as the recipient.
segments.s741-g37: چو is the conjunction 'when'. گشتاسپ is the subject (proper name). آن تخت رادید is a classical object construction: آن تخت 'that throne' plus the object marker را attached to the verb and written here fused as رادید (را + دید) = 'saw the throne'. گفت is the reporting verb 'said' (past); the provided sense list does not show the verbal sense, so that entry is treated as mismatched.
segments.s741-g38: که introduces a content clause 'that ...'. کار here means 'deed/affair' and بزرگان is 'the great ones' (here understood as ancestors/forebears or great men). نشاید is a modal expression 'it is not fitting/should not' and نهفت is a verbal/adjectival form meaning 'to be hidden/concealed' — together 'نشاید نهفت' = 'ought not to be concealed'.
segments.s741-g39: به جاماسپ گفت = 'he said to Jamasp': به marks the indirect object/recipient. گفت is the past reporting verb 'said' (the provided sense list is not the verbal sense). ای is the vocative particle 'O', followed by the epithet گرانمایه 'esteemed/noble' and مرد 'man' in address.
segments.s741-g40: فزونی literally 'increase/excess' is used idiomatically 'what more/what increase' (what additional merit). چه داری is a present interrogative 'what do you have/what is your possession' (داری = 2nd sg. 'you have'). به دین 'in/religion' marks the domain, and کارکرد is a deverbal noun 'doing/work/done' — together asking 'what more have you done in religious matters?'. Word order puts the question phrase before the domain and the deverbal noun at the end.
segments.s741-g121: This correlative pair 'چه ... چه ...' functions like English 'whether ... or ...'. 'ز' (here combined with the following clitic in the manuscript as 'زو') is the preposition 'from'. 'ایستاده' is a participial/past adjective 'standing/rose up' and 'مانده' is the past participle 'remained'. 'بجا' here qualifies 'مانده' as 'in place' (literally 'in the place'). Word order is typical for older Persian: prepositional phrase + predicate phrase; the two alternatives are balanced by the repeated 'چه'.
segments.s741-g122: 'بدیدی' is a past-tense seeing verb ('saw'); classical Persian often places the verb before its agent. 'به چشم سر' is an instrumental/adverbial phrase meaning 'by/with the eye of the head' — i.e. 'with his own eyes' — where 'به' functions adverbially ('by/with'), 'چشم' is 'eye' and 'سر' specifies 'head' (an idiomatic collocation). 'اخترگرا' at the end is the agent/noun 'astrologer'.
segments.s741-g123: The opening prepositional phrase 'ز شب' = 'from the night' (i.e. 'since nightfall'); 'نیز' adds 'also/too'. 'دیدی' is again a past-tense verb 'saw' and introduces a subordinate clause with 'که' = 'that'. 'چندی گذشت' = 'some time passed' where 'چندی' means 'for a while' and 'گذشت' is the past 'passed'.
segments.s741-g124: 'سپهر' is the subject 'the sky/heavens'. The phrase 'از بر خاک' is a poetic way to say 'from over the earth' ('از' + 'بر' + noun), and 'بر چند گشت' means 'turned several times' — 'بر' here is the preposition 'on/upon' and accompanies the verb 'گشت' (past of گشتن, 'to turn'), with 'چند' quantifying 'several/many times'. The sentence uses inverted/poetic order but reads as 'the sky, from above the earth, turned several times.'
segments.s741-g125: 'ازان تختها' = 'from those thrones' (ازان = older/poetic 'از آن'). The phrase 'چند زرین' is a nominal phrase 'several golden [ones]'. The final word 'بدی' in this context functions as an archaic/expletive copular or verbal element meaning roughly 'there were' or 'existed' (not the modern sense 'evil'); it is a poetic usage and the exact lexical sense is irregular here.
segments.s741-g126: 'چه مایه' is an exclamatory quantifier meaning 'what an amount' or 'how much', with 'مایه' here = 'amount/quantity'. 'ز زر گوهر آگین' is a genitive chain 'of gold, gem‑bright jewels' (ز = 'of/from', زر = 'gold', گوهر = 'gem', آگین qualifies 'bright/shining'). The final 'بدی' again acts as an archaic verbal/copular element indicating existence or provision and is used poetically.
segments.s741-g127: 'شمارش' is the noun 'counting/reckoning'. 'ندانست' is the negative past form meaning 'did not know' (classical formation) and here carries the sense 'did not know' or 'knew not how'. 'کردن' is the light verb 'to do' used with شمارش (i.e. 'to do the counting'), and 'کسی' is the indefinite 'anyone/no one' — together the phrase means 'no one knew how to make the count' or 'no one could reckon the tally.'
segments.s741-g128: 'اگر' introduces a concessive conditional 'if'. 'چند' here = 'any (in some amount)/some' and 'بودیش' is an archaic third‑person impersonal/past existence ('there was' / 'it was'); 'دانش' = 'knowledge' and 'بسی' = 'much/enough' (here negated by context: 'if there was any knowledge, it was but little'). The clause means roughly 'if there was any knowledge at all, it was small.'
segments.s741-g73: ورا (vora) is an indirect-object/locative pronoun 'to him' placed before the verb; درگر (dargar, archaic for دگر) means 'other/another' modifying the implied people who 'came.' آمد is the past tense 'came', and the place phrases ز روم و ز چین follow the verb with the poetic preposition ز 'from' (here repeated before چین). Word order here is pronoun + modifier + verb + source phrases (typical of epic Persian).
segments.s741-g74: ز introduces a coordinated list of origins: 'from Makrān and Baghdad and the land of Iran.' In Persian the single preposition ز at the start governs all following coordinated nouns (مکران و بغداد و ایران‌زمین). The sequence simply enumerates place-names.
segments.s741-g75: The number phrase هزار و صد و بیست ('one thousand and one hundred and twenty') precedes the noun استاد 'master(s).' بود is the past copula 'was/were' (here marking existence in the past). Persian places numeral + noun before the verb, so the clause literally reads 'one thousand one hundred twenty masters were.'
segments.s741-g76: که introduces a relative clause modifying استاد. The clause ڪردار آن تختشان یادبود literally orders as 'the conduct of that their throne memorial-was' — یادبود functions as a predicative noun 'a memorial/commemoration.' Note the possessive suffix -شان on تخت ('their throne') and the omission of an explicit copula inside the relative clause (typical in Persian: noun + noun can serve predicatively).
segments.s741-g77: که again begins a relative clause. او را is the definite object ('it' or 'him' depending on referent) marked by the object marker را and placed before the verb; بنا is a noun 'building/edifice' specifying what was made. شاه گشتاسپ is the subject (King Gushtasp) and کرد is the past-tense 'did/made' — together: 'King Gushtasp made/built it.'
segments.s741-g78: برای here opens a short coordinating phrase and pairs with به تدبیر 'by the counsel/plan of'; به is the instrumental/prepositional use 'by/with' and تدبیر means 'plan, counsel.' جاماسپ is the agent and کرد is again the past 'did' — so 'and by the counsel of Jamasp it was done' (i.e. Jamasp planned/designed it). The construction به تدبیر X indicates instrument/agency.
segments.s741-g79: ابا is an archaic/formulaic particle here introducing the distribution 'for/with each' (contextually 'for each one'). هریکی 'each one' is the distributive, followed by مرد شاگرد 'male pupils' (noun + modifier) and then the numeral سی 'thirty' (the numeral follows the noun in this epic formula). The clause means 'for each there were thirty male pupils.'
segments.s741-g80: ز introduces the list of origins for those pupils: 'from Rūm and Baghdād and Pārs (Persia).' As above, the single ز governs the coordinated place-nouns connected by و.
segments.s741-g137: دو تخت (two thrones) is the subject phrase; از بر here is a poetic combination placing the two thrones 'on/above' another throne (از functions as a poetic locative and بر = on/upon). تخت پرمایه is a genitival/compound description 'a richly endowed throne,' and بود is the past copula 'was/stood.' Word order (subject + locative + predicate) is typical of epic Persian.
segments.s741-g138: ز گوهر بسی مایه بر مایه بود is a verbless/descriptive clause with بود as the main verb 'there was.' ز = 'of/from' marks material (gems), گوهر = 'gems,' بسی functions adverbially 'much/abundant,' and مایه بر مایه 'layer upon layer (wealth/substance)' expresses piling; بود completes as past 'there was.'
segments.s741-g139: کهین تخت را places 'the ancient throne' as the definite object/topic with را; نام بد میش سار is a naming phrase — the sequence supplies the name (بد میش سار) rather than a predicative adjective. There is no explicit copula here: the phrase functions as 'the ancient throne was named Bad-Mīsh-Sār.'
segments.s741-g140: سر میش is a genitive phrase 'the head of a sheep' (سر = top/head, modified by میش = ewe). بودی is the past copula 'was.' برو بر نگار is a short locative phrase 'upon/over the ornament/image' (برو used poetically as a locative particle and بر = on; نگار here names the decorated object). Together the clause says the sheep's head was situated on the ornament.
segments.s741-g141: مهین تخت را places 'the majestic/throne' as the definite object with را; راخواندی is the 2nd-person past verb 'you called/named' (poetic past ending -دی), and لاژورد is the name/predicative noun 'lapis-lazuli.' So the clause means 'you named the majestic throne Lajvard.'
segments.s741-g142: که introduces a relative clause 'which/that.' هرگز نبودی is a negative past 'never was,' and برو باد و گرد is a compact locative/instrumental phrase meaning 'to/by wind and dust' (برو used poetically as locative/agentive + باد 'wind' and گرد 'dust'). The whole clause qualifies the throne as never being exposed to wind and dust.
segments.s741-g143: سه دیگر ('three others') is the subject; سراسر ز پیروزه بود is a predicate: سراسر 'throughout/entirely,' ز = 'of,' پیروزه = 'turquoise,' and بود is the past copula 'was.' The clause literally says the three others were entirely made of turquoise.
segments.s741-g144: بدو 'to it/unto them' (prepositional/directional), هر که 'whoever,' که is the relative particle 'who/that,' and دیدیش is past 'saw it' where the final -ش is an object pronoun referring back to the thrones; دلسوزه بود predicates 'was lovesick/grief-stricken' with بود as the past copula. The sentence means 'whoever saw them became lovesick.'
segments.s741-g9: This phrase names the person: یکی مرد بد = 'a certain/one bad man' (یکی used here as 'a certain'); adjective بد follows مرد as in Persian. در + location follows: در دماوند کوه = 'in Mount Damavand' — note the poetic inverted order دماوند کوه instead of modern کوه دماوند.
segments.s741-g10: که introduces a relative clause 'who/that'. شاهش is شاه + possessive -ش, 'his king'. جدا is adverb 'apart/aside'. داشتی is an archaic/past form meaning 'had/kept' (past third-person sense); verb appears after the possessed noun, a common poetic order. ازگروه = 'from the group/company'.
segments.s741-g11: کجا here is a poetic/deictic introducer ('there/now' or 'where' in narrative), leading into the proper name. جهن برزین is a two-part proper name. بدی نام اوی literally 'of bad name his' = 'ill‑famed' (بدی modifies نام) and اوی is the possessive 'his'. The whole segment gives 'Jahan Barzin, ill‑famed by name'.
segments.s741-g12: رسیده is the perfect/past participle 'has arrived/come'. بهر + کشوری = 'for/to the sake of a realm' (بهر = for/toward, کشوری = of the realm/country). کام اوی = 'his desire' (کام = desire; اوی = his). Together: 'he had arrived intent on a realm to satisfy his desire.'
segments.s741-g13: یکی نامور شاه را تخت ساخت — 'یکی نامور' = 'a certain/famed one' modifying شاه; را is the topical/definite marker attached to شاه in this poetic order. تخت ساخت = 'built/made a throne' (تخت is the object, ساخت is past 'built').
segments.s741-g14: گهر گرد بر گرد او = 'gems/pearls around about him' — گهر 'gems', the repeated گرد + بر گرد is a poetic stacking meaning 'round/around'. در نشاخت uses در ('in/within') with نشاخت, an archaic noun meaning 'discord' or 'strife'; in context the clause states that no discord was raised in his circle (the line uses the archaic noun to comment on the state of his court).
segments.s741-g15: که introduces an explanatory clause 'for/that'. شاه آفریدون is the subject 'King Afrīdūn'. بدوشاد is an archaic adjectival form meaning 'joyful' or 'glad' here, and بود is the past copula 'was'. So: 'that King Afrīdūn was joyful.'
segments.s741-g16: که = 'for/because'. آن تخت = 'that throne'. پرمایه is an adjective 'well‑endowed / rich in resources' and آزاد here means 'noble/generous' (an honorific adjective often applied to persons or things); بود is 'was' — 'for that throne was richly endowed and noble.'
segments.s741-g57: بدین گونه بد is a frozen adverbial phrase meaning “in this bad/ill manner.” Here the text is split into ب + دین + گونه + بد; ب is the older verbal/relational prefix (used in poetry), گونه = “manner/way,” and بد = adverb “badly/ill.” تا + سرِ اردشیر follows as a limit phrase: تا (as far as/until) + سر (literally “head,” used here to mean ‘up to’ or ‘as far as’ and referring to the person Ardashir). The whole chunk sets the scope (how ill things proceeded, extending even to Ardashir).
segments.s741-g58: کجا گشته بد نام آن تخت پیر is an interrogative/exclamatory clause: کجا = “where”; گشته is the verbal participle/verb here meaning “has become / was turned” (classical/poetic perfective sense); بد نام is a compound adjective “ill‑named / ill‑famed” (بد qualifying نام in the sense of reputation); آن تخت پیر = “that old/ancient throne.” The order is poetic (verb before adjective phrase), giving: “where had that old throne become ill‑famed?”
segments.s741-g59: ازان تخت جایی نشانی نیافت is a straightforward past action: ازان = “from that / of that,” تخت = “throne,” جایی نشانی = “any trace/place/sign” (جایی here = any place/anywhere and نشانی = trace/sign), and نیافت is the past negative of یافتن (“he did not find”). Together: “He found no trace of that throne there.”
segments.s741-g60: بران آرزو سوی دیگر شتاف = a compact clause: بران (بر + آن) = “thereupon / with that (on that matter),” آرزو = “desire/wish,” سوی دیگر = “to another direction / elsewhere,” and شتاف is the past of شتابیدن (“he hurried / hastened”). Word order is subjectless and compressed: “Thereupon, with that desire, he hastened elsewhere.”
segments.s741-g61: بمرد او و آن تخت ازو بازماند uses an archaic/poetic verbal form: بمرد here functions as the past narration “he died” (the prefix بـ is a classical/poetic form attached to the verb). او = “he,” و = “and,” آن تخت = “that throne,” ازو = “from him/away from him,” and بازماند is a past form meaning “remained / was left behind.” The clause says: “He died, and that throne remained away from him.”
segments.s741-g62: ازان پس که کام بزرگی براند is a temporal/explanatory clause: ازان پس = “from that time / thereafter,” که introduces the subordinate content, کام بزرگی = “a great desire/wish,” and براند is an older/poetic form of a verb meaning here “to attain/bring about” (classically related to راندن/رَفتن usages but idiomatically used with کام to mean fulfillment). So the clause means “from that time, after (he) had brought about/attained a great desire.”
segments.s741-g63: بدین گونه بد تا به پرویزشاه repeats the adverbial frame from above: بدین گونه بد = “in this bad/ill manner,” تا = “until,” به = “to/toward,” and پرویزشاه = the name/title “Parvizshah (King Parviz).” The phrase sets the temporal/directional limit: “until it reached Parvizshah.” Note the poetic split of بدین into ب + دین in the supplied tokens; functionally it reads “بدین” = “in this way.”
segments.s741-g64: رسید آن گرامی سزاوار گاه is a compact predicate: رسید = “he arrived / it reached / came to pass,” آن گرامی = “that honorable/esteemed one,” سزاوار = “fit, deserving,” and گاه = “time/occasion.” Together this reads “that honored one reached (or arrived at) the proper/fitting time,” i.e. the appropriate moment came for him. The verb precedes the noun phrase in typical epic/poetic order.
segments.s741-g97: برو is the imperative 'go'. The enclitic بش here functions as an older/poetic allative clitic 'to him/for him' (equivalent to بهِ او), so برو بش = 'go to him'. زرین is an adjective 'golden' modifying the following numeral phrase; صد و چل هزار is the older numeric order for 'one hundred and forty thousand' (صد = 100, چل = archaic form of چهل = 40, هزار = thousand).
segments.s741-g98: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from/of/with' used to introduce the material or ornamentation. پیروزه = 'turquoise' (the stone or turquoise inlay). بر زر = 'upon gold' (بر = on/upon, زر = gold). کرده نگار is a compound: کرده is the past participle 'made/done' and نگار here means an ornament/pattern/picture (i.e. a decorative object) — together 'a piece made/ornamented'. The whole segment describes an ornament set with turquoise on gold.
segments.s741-g99: همه 'all' is the quantifier for نقره 'silver'. خام 'raw/crude' qualifies the silver. بد میخ literally 'bad nail(s)' — میخ = nail/peg (metal studs), بد = bad; Persian here omits an explicit copula, so the adjectives are predicative. The final بش again marks beneficiary/possession 'for him' (poetic).
segments.s741-g100: یکی begins the numeric specification meaning 'one' / 'each'. به مثقال marks the unit of weight (here 'in mithqals'); به is the preposition used to introduce the measure. با شست و شش gives the remainder of the number: با = 'with' and شست و شش is the old numeric expression for 'sixty and six' (i.e. +66), so the whole phrase yields 'one hundred and sixty‑six mithqals'. The verb 'weighed' or similar is implied.
segments.s741-g101: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. اندر = 'inside/within'. بره خور is a compound noun 'lamb‑eater' (بره = lamb; خور = eater, present stem 'eats') — a descriptive compound. نهادی here functions as a past action 'you placed/you set' (poetic past of نهادن). چراغ is the direct object 'a lamp'. The clause is a temporal subordinate: 'When you set a lamp inside the lamb‑eater...'.
segments.s741-g102: پسش = 'behind it' (پس + third‑person clitic). دشت 'plain' is the locative noun; بودی is the past copula 'was'. The clause order 'پسش دشت بودی' places the locative phrase before the verb in typical poetic inversion: 'behind it was a plain'. و links the next locative: در پیش باغ = 'and before it (was) a garden' (در used as locative + پیش 'in front of' + باغ 'garden').
segments.s741-g103: چوخورشید is the fused temporal phrase 'when the sun...'. درشیرگشتی is a poetic compound meaning 'entered the Lion / became lion‑like' (here astrophorical: the sun entering the zodiacal sign Leo), treated as a past/instant event. درشت is the adjective 'big/large' describing the ensuing state ('it became large/great'). The segment is a temporal clause describing the sun's position and its effect.
segments.s741-g104: مرآن is the older accusative/possessive form 'me/my' (used here to mark possession: 'my'). تخت را = 'the throne' with the object/definite marker را. سوی او 'toward him' (سوی = toward, او = him/her). بود is the past copula 'was'. پشت 'back' appears sentence‑finally so the clause reads 'the back of my throne was toward him' (i.e. the throne faced away from him).
segments.s741-g89: بلندیش is a noun formed from بلند with the possessive enclitic -ش (its/their) — literally “its height.” پنجاه و صد is a poetic number-ordering meaning  fifty-and-hundred = 150 (classical inversion of صد و پنجاه). The conjunction و simply links the two parts of the numeral; رش here names the unit (given in the text as رش) and follows the number.
segments.s741-g90: چنان بد که is a result clause: چنان … که = “so … that.” Here بد is an archaic/poetic form of بود (“was”), so the phrase means “it was so (high) that …”. بر ابر سودی سرش is a prepositional phrase: بر + ابر = “on/upon a cloud,” سودی (beneficent/profitable) modifies ابر, and سر + -ش = “its/top.” The enclitic -ش marks possession (‘its head/top’).
segments.s741-g91: همان is the demonstrative 'that very' / 'the same.' شاه رش here forms a compound name/term (Shah‑rash or an item called 'شاه رش'); هر + رشی = 'each rishi' (هر before a noun = 'each/every'). ز (poetic form of از) means 'from/of,' and the sequence زو سه رش literally gives 'from it three rash' — the verb is omitted (understood 'there were' or 'counted'), a common elliptical construction in poetry.
segments.s741-g92: کزان = از + آن (from which/whence) introduces a relative/origin phrase. سر is the noun 'head/top' and بدیدی is the past form (here meaning 'was seen' / 'one saw'); the clause therefore expresses that 'from which the head was seen.' بن کشورش is a locative/genitive phrase: بن = 'base/bottom' and کشور + -ش = 'his realm/land.' Word order is typical: origin (کزان), subject/thing seen (سر), verb (بدیدی), then location (بن کشورش).
segments.s741-g93: بسی here works as an intensifier meaning 'many' or 'frequently.' روز در ماه literally 'days in the month' = 'days of the month.' هر بامداد is an adverbial phrase 'every morning' (هر + بامداد). The sequence gives a temporal frame: 'many days in the month, every morning…'.
segments.s741-g94: یکی فرش = 'one carpet' (یکی used as a numeral/indefinite). بودی is a past copula 'was' (classical past of بودن). به دیگر نهاد = 'placed onto another' where نهاد is the past (3rd‑sg) of نهادن ('to put/place') and به دیگر ('upon another') is the goal/target. The verb نهاد carries the action; the clause order (object/phrase + verb) is typical Persian.
segments.s741-g95: همان تخت = 'that very throne.' به + دوازده + لخت is a unit-of-count/measure phrase 'in twelve laks/parts' (به used with a number to indicate division or grouping). لخت here is an archaic/poetic noun (literally 'bare/plain' in the dictionary) and is used in context as 'parts/layers/sections' (a poetic usage). بود is the past copula 'was.'
segments.s741-g96: جهانی سراسر همه sets up the scope: جهانی ('worldly'/'the world'), سراسر ('throughout'), همه ('all/every'). تخت بود is the predicate 'was throne(s)' — in effect saying the world was entirely filled with/like thrones. Note the adjective/adverbial chain (جهانی سراسر همه) intensifies the universality before the final copular clause.
segments.s741-g113: همان is a demonstrative 'that/those' placed before the noun for emphasis; گوی here means 'orb/sphere' (not 'saying'); زرین and سیمین are adjectives 'golden' and 'silvery' modifying گوی; هزار quantifies them ('a thousand') and follows the descriptive phrase in this poetic word order.
segments.s741-g114: بر آتش is a prepositional phrase 'upon the fire'; همی‌تافتی is an archaic/imperfective verbal form of تافتن with the prefix همی- indicating continuous or habitual aspect ('was shining'); the verb appears after the locative phrase as in SOV order of Persian poetry; جامه‌دار is an epithet/vocative 'robed' or 'garment-wearer' addressing someone.
segments.s741-g115: به مثقال is a measure phrase 'by/at the mithqāl' where به functions as 'by' indicating a rate; ازان ('of those' / 'of that') combines with هریکی 'each one' to mean 'each of those'; پانصد is the numeral 'five hundred' giving the quantity of each.
segments.s741-g116: کز introduces a relative clause meaning 'which/from which'; آتش is the source 'from the fire'; شدی is the verb 'became' (from شدن) describing change of state; سرخ 'red' is the result; همچون 'as/like' introduces the simile and بسد (here verbal) means 'it suffices' / 'is enough' used idiomatically to indicate the degree of redness — overall: 'which from the fire became red, as if it were enough.'
segments.s741-g117: یکی نیمه زو means 'one half of it' (زو is a contracted poetic 'از + او' = 'of it'); اندر آتش is 'into the fire'; the final بدی here is an archaic past second‑person verb 'you gave/placed' (دادی) placed at the end, as is normal in Persian syntax. Note: the provided lexical senses list for بدی did not include the verbal sense, so it is marked unavailable.
segments.s741-g118: دگر = 'the other (one)'; پیش گردان = 'before/in front of the troop' where گردان is a noun 'troop/division' and پیش places it spatially; سرکش qualifies گردان as 'headstrong/rebellious'; the clause ends with بدی (again the past 'you gave'), so the whole means 'you gave the other to the headstrong troop in front.' The verbal sense of بدی is not listed in the provided senses.
segments.s741-g119: شمار ستاره is 'the number of stars' (count/total) followed by a poetic enumeration ده و دو و هفت 'ten and two and seven' (i.e. 19); the conjunction و links the numerals in sequence, a typical enumerative pattern in Persian verse.
segments.s741-g120: همان ماه تابان = 'that same shining moon' (demonstrative + noun + participial adjective); ببرجی is an adjectival/locative form meaning 'in the sign/house (of the zodiac)' (ب- + برج + -ی), i.e. astrological placement; که رفت is a relative clause 'which went/left/passed' with رفت as the past-tense verb at the end.
segments.s741-g25: This is a nominal enumeration: 'یکی' (one) introduces the first of the three things; the noun 'تخت' (throne) follows in normal Persian noun order. 'و آن گرزه گاوسار' links with 'and that' + a noun phrase: 'گرزه' (mace) followed by the proper name 'گاوسار' (Gausar) as an apposition/epithet (the mace called Gausar).
segments.s741-g26: 'که' is a relative pronoun 'which/that' introducing the relative clause describing the mace. 'ماندست' is an archaic/poetic perfect/present-tense-stative meaning 'remains / has remained'. 'زو' is written here as two items ('ز' + 'و') and functions as 'from him' (poetic 'ز' = from + 'او' = him). 'در جهان یادگار' is a prepositional phrase 'in the world as a memorial', with 'یادگار' as predicate noun.
segments.s741-g27: 'سدیگر' (the other/another) introduces the second item. 'کجا' here is a locative/poetic marker meaning 'where' or 'at which place', and 'هفت چشمه گهر' is a compound noun phrase 'seven jewel-springs' (چشمه = spring, گهر = gem/jewel) describing that place or thing.
segments.s741-g28: 'همی‌خواندی' is an archaic/habitual form meaning 'used to call / kept calling' (a repeated/past-habit sense). 'نام او' = 'his name' and 'دادگر' is an epithet/title (here used as the name 'Dadgar' = 'the Just'). The clause states that people habitually called him by that name.
segments.s741-g29: 'چو' = 'when' introduces a temporal clause. 'ایرج بشد' uses the archaic past/preterite 'بشد' = 'became' (it became/it happened — here 'Iraj became [king]'). 'زو' again appears as 'ز' + 'و' meaning 'from him'; 'بماند' is a present/past-stative 'remained' (to remain), and 'این سه چیز' is the direct object 'these three things' which is what remained.
segments.s741-g30: This clause comments on the effect: 'همان' = 'the same' (those same things). 'شاد بد' is an archaic contraction where 'بد' stands for 'بود' (was), so 'شاد بد' = 'was glad/was pleased'. 'زو' = 'از او' / 'because of it' (from it), and 'منوچهر نیز' = 'Manuchehr also'. The whole means Manuchehr too was happy because of those same things.
segments.s741-g31: 'هر آنکس که' = 'whichever person who / whoever' introduces a conditional/relative clause. 'او' is the resumptive pronoun 'he' (referring back to 'هر آنکس'). 'تاج شاهی' = 'the crown of kingship' (تاج = crown, شاهی = kingship). 'بسود' is a dative/adverbial expression 'to the advantage / for the profit of' (i.e. 'if the crown proved advantageous to him').
segments.s741-g32: 'بران' = 'upon that / to that' (referring back to the throne), 'تخت' = 'throne'. 'چیزی' = 'something' and 'همی‌برفزود' is an archaic/habitual verb form meaning 'would add / increased' (literally 'kept adding' or 'added'). Together: such a person would add something to that throne.
segments.s741-g161: Word order is emphatic/classical: بدو (to it) is an indirect object placed before the verb; کرده is the perfective participle 'has made' used as an auxiliary/causative; پیدانشان is a compound notion 'their being made known / their appearance' (پیدان + شان). سپهر (the subject) comes at the end for poetic emphasis: literally 'to it has made known their appearance the heavens.'
segments.s741-g162: چو here means 'like' (a simile). The list that follows names celestial/heroic figures in parallel: بهرام and کیوان are proper/astral names, the repeated و simply links items, then چون introduces another simile 'as' before ماه و مهر. The sequence is a string of comparisons describing how they appear.
segments.s741-g163: ز introduces a source 'from' (poetic register). The sequence names celestial bodies/entities joined by و. گردنده modifies ماه ('the turning/revolving moon'), with ز before گردنده indicating 'from the revolving moon'. The whole phrase lists sources/origins.
segments.s741-g164: پدیدار here is a noun/nominal notion 'manifestation / making visible' and is governed by کرده, where کرده functions as the perfective 'has made' (causative). ز هر دستگاه = 'from (or in) every system/order' — ز can mark origin or scope, and هر دستگاه specifies 'every arrangement/constellation'.
segments.s741-g165: هم is an additive/emphatic particle 'also' or 'even'. از هفت کشور = 'from / over the seven countries/lands' (classical ordering allows از to indicate provenance or range). برو بر نشان: برو here is a classical/poetic verb form related to 'to be placed / to go' and بر نشان means 'as a sign/on a token' — the phrase means 'he set/ placed as a token over the seven lands.'
segments.s741-g166: ز marks origin/source again: 'from the dehqān' (دهقان here refers to the landed gentry/dihqān). و از رزم گردنکشان adds a second source 'and from the battle of the rebels' (گردنکشان is a plural substantive meaning the insurgent/proud ones); the phrase lists social/military origins connected to the token mentioned before.
segments.s741-g167: برو بر نشان repeats the verbal phrase 'set/placed as a sign'; چل و هشت شاه is a classical numeric phrase 'forty and eight kings' = 'forty‑eight kings' where چل (forty) is joined by و هشت (and eight) to شاه (kings). The order places the numeral(s) before the noun, as is normal in Persian.
segments.s741-g168: پدیدار کرده again is the causative/perfective cluster 'has made manifest / has revealed'. سر تاج = 'the head/top of the crown' (سر = top/summit) and و گاه = 'and the time/season' — the two nouns are the things made manifest; classical Persian often places the verb cluster before or after such noun phrases for metrical reasons.
segments.s741-g145: ازین تا بدان پایه بودی چهار is an older/inverted Persian word order: از + این (ازین) 'from here' … تا بدان 'to that' mark the span, followed by the noun پایه 'pedestal'; بودی is an archaic past plural/existential 'there were'. The numeral چهار appears after the predicate (poetic inversion) and counts the preceding noun.
segments.s741-g146: همه پایه زرین و گوهرنگار: همه 'all' functions as a determiner for پایه. Persian adjectives follow the noun, so پایه زرین 'golden pedestal' and گوهرنگار 'gem‑adorned' are postpositive modifiers describing the pedestals.
segments.s741-g147: هرآنکس که introduces a relative clause 'whoever/anyone that…'. دهقان بد و زیردست is a coordinated description (the bad/dehgān and the underling); there is no ezāfe in this older style but the nouns and adjectives behave like a modern noun phrase.
segments.s741-g148: ورا is an indirect-object/adverbial 'to him/for him' (here: 'for anyone so seen'). میش سر is a compound image literally 'sheep-head' (used here as a sheepskin/sheepskin‑seat). بود is past 'was' and جای نشست 'seat/place of sitting' is the predicate; word order places the predicate elements after بود in poetic style.
segments.s741-g149: سواران ناباک روز نبرد is a noun phrase: سواران 'horsemen' modified by the adjective ناباک 'virtuous/pure' and the temporal/genitive phrase روز نبرد 'the day of battle' (i.e. 'horsemen of battle‑day'). Adjectives follow the noun; the whole phrase names the figures shown.
segments.s741-g150: شدندی is an archaic past plural meaning 'they were made/appeared' (here: 'were depicted/placed'). بران = بر + آن 'upon that' marks the location; گنبد لاژورد 'the lapis‑lazuli dome' is the locative target. The verb is plural matching the plural subject in the previous segment.
segments.s741-g151: به پیروزه بر جای is a locative prepositional phrase: به پیروزه 'on/at the turquoise (inlay)' and بر جای 'on the seat/place'; دستور بود is an existential statement 'there was an order/arrangement'. In older Persian the prepositions and word order are somewhat more compressed than modern prose.
segments.s741-g152: که introduces the content/explanation of that دستور. از here carries a causal/agentive sense 'because of/from'; کدخداییش is کدخدا + possessive/abstractive suffix -یش meaning 'his being a kâdkhudâ / his kâdkhudâ‑ship'; رنجور بود is the predicate 'was distressed/afflicted'. The clause explains the substance of the rule.
segments.s741-g169: برو here functions as a poetically used imperative/interjection ('behold' / 'go, see') calling attention to what follows. بافته is a past participle 'woven' modifying تاج; the phrase places the participial modifier before the noun in a compact poetic order. تاجِ شاهنشهان = 'the crown of the emperors' (شاهنشهان is a possessive plural/collective title 'the emperors').
segments.s741-g170: چنان is a demonstrative/adverb 'such'; جامه is the noun 'garment/robe' governed by that demonstrative. هرگز is the negative adverb 'never'. نبد is an archaic/poetic past negative (≈ نبود, 'was not / did not exist'); درجهان is the locative phrase 'in the world' (here written together), giving the scope of the negation.
segments.s741-g171: به چین دریکی = 'in/at one fold' (به + چین + دریکی) — به is the preposition indicating location, چین 'fold' and دریکی 'in one/together' form a locative phrase. مرد بد بی‌همال is the subject: مرد 'man', بد here an attributive/epithet (poetic; 'notable/strong' or simply 'a man' with negative/old sense), and بی‌همال 'unceasing/indefatigable', so 'an unceasing/tireless man.'
segments.s741-g172: همی‌بافت is the imperfective/continuous past 'he was weaving' (همی- marks ongoing action). آن جامه را is the direct object ('that garment' with the object marker را). راهفت here should be read as two words را هفت ('ra' object marker + هفت 'seven') in the original manuscript, so the phrase reads '... را هفت سال' = 'for seven years'; سال is 'year'.
segments.s741-g173: سرسال نو is a time expression 'the start of the new year' (سرسال = beginning of the year, نو = new). هرمز فرودین comprises proper names/titles placed together: هرمز (here a proper name/ divine name) and فرودین (Farvardin — a name or the month-name); together they indicate who arrives at this time.
segments.s741-g174: بیامد is simple past 'came' (3rd sing.). بر is the preposition here meaning 'to/unto' (marking motion toward someone); شاه is the indirect object 'the king'; ایران‌زمین is the title 'land of Iran' specifying which king. The clause says that the named visitor came into the king's presence.
segments.s741-g175: ببرد is the past form of بردن ('to carry/take') used here as 'brought/placed.' The verb precedes its object in this poetic order: 'ببرد آن کیی فرش نزدیک شاه' = 'he carried/placed that Kayanian carpet near the king.' کیی (Kayanian) qualifies فرش as belonging to the Kayanian lineage.
segments.s741-g176: گران مایگان is a nominal phrase 'the noble/precious Mâyagân' (گران = precious/noble modifying مایگان — here a plural group, likely priests or important people). برگرفتند is the past plural 'they took up / lifted' (used idiomatically with راه to mean 'they took the road' / 'they set out'). راه is the object/goal 'the road/way.'
segments.s741-g177: بگسترد is the past 3rd‑singular of گسترَدن with the archaic/poetic prefix بـ-, meaning 'he spread/laid out'. روز نو is an adverbial temporal phrase 'the new day' (i.e. 'on the new day'). آن جامه را is the definite object 'that garment' with را marking it; word order here places the verb first (poetic inversion), then the time phrase, then the object.
segments.s741-g178: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. شادی is the noun 'joy/celebration'. جداکرد is the compound verb جدا کردن in past 3rd‑singular 'he separated/removed'. خودکامه را is the definite object 'the self‑willed/autocratic one' with را; the clause literally says 'he separated the self‑willed one from joy.'
segments.s741-g179: بران here functions as a locative/adverbial particle meaning roughly 'upon that' (poetic usage) preceding جامه, the object 'that garment'. بر مجلس is the prepositional phrase 'upon the assembly/hall/court'. آراستند is past 3rd‑plural of آراستن 'they adorned/arranged', so the clause means 'they adorned/arranged that garment upon the assembly.'
segments.s741-g180: نوازنده is the agent noun 'the musician/player'. رود here denotes the instrument (rūd, a stringed instrument), not the river. و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. می‌خواستند is the imperfect/past habitual 'they wanted/wished' (indicating desire or intention); in narrative it often introduces what they meant or were about to do.
segments.s741-g181: همی is an archaic/adverbial particle used in epic Persian to mark continuous or emphatic action ('kept/continually'). آفرین is the exclamation 'bravo!/afarin!'. خواند is past 3rd‑singular of خواندن; here it means 'uttered/called out' (i.e. cried the exclamation). سرکش is the subject 'the unruly/headstrong one' placed after the verb by poetic inversion. به رود is the phrase 'to the rud' (i.e. addressing or praising the instrument/player).
segments.s741-g182: شهنشاه را is the definite object 'the shah/emperor' marked by را. داد is past 'gave' (here 'offered/paid'). چندی functions adverbially 'for a while' or 'for some time'. درود means 'greeting(s)' or 'salutation(s)'. Together: '(he) gave the emperor greetings for a short while.'
segments.s741-g183: بزرگان is the plural subject 'the great ones/nobles'. برو here appears as a locative/particle meaning roughly 'thereupon' (poetic usage—see note), followed by گوهر 'jewels/gems' as the object. افشاندند is past 3rd‑plural 'they scattered/sprinkled'. The clause thus means 'the nobles then scattered gems.' (If the printed lemma for برو seems literal 'go', here its function is poetic locative; no exact sense index was provided that matches that usage.)
segments.s741-g184: که introduces the following explanatory/result clause 'so that/that'. فرش بزرگش is 'his/their great carpet' (بزرگ 'great' + enclitic ش 'his/its'). همی‌خواندند is past‑habitual 3rd‑plural 'they were singing/they sang' or 'they chanted' — here idiomatically 'they sang (the praises of) his great carpet' or 'they sang about the great carpet', with the subject elided (continuing reference to the assembly/people).
